text: The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection for splenic flexure mobilization, entering below the inferior mesenteric vein while preserving retroperitoneal structures. A swab is placed for protection, and the inferior mesenteric vein is ligated and divided. Entry into the lesser sac is achieved above the pancreas, confirmed by visualizing the posterior wall of the stomach. The surgeon then attempts to gain entry into the lesser sac from above the transverse colon.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the transverse mesocolon, avoiding disruption to the transverse mesocolon in a patient with increased adipose deposition and fused planes. The transverse colon is retracted cranially, revealing patchy ischemia and a significant defect in the transverse mesocolon, indicating disruption of the blood supply.

The transverse colon is mobilized up to the hepatic flexure, revealing the extent of ischemia. The specimen is visibly unable to reach the pelvis, indicating the need for resection proximal to the ischemic area.

The surgeon performs medial to lateral dissection to mobilize the entire left colon, taking the inferior mesenteric artery. A sizer is placed in the rectum to ensure the stapling device will pass. The rectum is prepared for transection, and the specimen is extracted following right colon mobilization. The excised specimen is removed, and the colonic conduit is reintroduced into the abdomen through a glove port with an anvil attached to the colonic end.

The surgeon focuses on the terminal ileum, identifying an avascular plane near the ileocolic pedicle. A space is created transmesenterically in this avascular plane, carefully avoiding disruption of significant vasculature, including the ileocolic pedicle. The peritoneal component of the mesentery is extended to create a void for the conduit to pass through.

The surgeon ensures a safe, bloodless passage to the opposite peritoneal side of the mesentery. The terminal ileum is fully mobilized from lateral attachments, and the defect is enlarged to facilitate the passage of the conduit and anvil through the created defect.

The conduit is visualized passing safely down to the pelvis, allowing for a tension-free anastomosis. A circular stapler gun is fired up the rectum, with a spike introduced to perform the anastomosis, ensuring the conduit is correctly oriented to prevent twisting. An air leak test is conducted to verify the integrity of the anastomosis.
